title: Pasta e fagioli
subtitle: Italian pasta and borlotti bean soup
excerpt: A thick, hearty soup of borlotti beans, small pasta, rosemary, garlic, pancetta, and tomato, with a parmesan rind simmered in the broth for depth. Half the beans are mashed to give the soup its dense, starchy body. The canonical Italian peasant dish: filling, economical, and deeply savoury.
sourceNotes: Pasta e fagioli (pasta and beans) is one of the foundational dishes of Italian cucina povera and appears in cookbooks from the eighteenth century. It is associated with the Veneto, Lazio, and Campania (regions with strong bean-growing traditions) and varies considerably by region (the Venetian version is more liquid, the Roman version thicker). The technique of mashing half the beans to thicken the broth is universal. It was originally a meatless dish; pancetta or lardons are a later addition.

content.text: The parmesan rind is the secret ingredient. It adds no flavour you can point to directly, but it gives the broth a savoury depth that plain beans and stock cannot achieve. Save parmesan rinds in a bag in the freezer (they will last indefinitely) and add one whenever a bean soup or braise needs an extra dimension. Remove it before serving.

The soup should be thick enough that the pasta does not sink to the bottom: when you stir it, the pasta should move through resistance. Use small pasta (ditalini, elbow macaroni, or broken spaghetti) and cook it in the soup rather than separately, so the starch it releases thickens the broth further and the pasta absorbs the bean flavour. This means the soup will thicken substantially on standing.

items.name: Dried pasta
items.prepNote: small shapes, ditalini, elbow, or broken spaghetti

items.name: Parmesan
items.prepNote: grated to serve, plus a rind for the broth if available

content.text: Build the base and simmer the beans

Heat the {{olive-oil}} in a large casserole over medium heat. Add the {{pancetta}} and cook for 4 minutes until the fat renders. Add the {{onion}} and {{celery}} and cook for 8 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} and {{rosemary}} and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and cook for 3 minutes. Add the {{borlotti-beans}}, {{stock-chicken}}, and parmesan rind if using. Bring to a simmer and cook for 15 minutes.
Mash half the beans and cook the pasta

Remove and discard the {{rosemary}} sprigs and parmesan rind. Remove about half the beans with a slotted spoon and mash them with a fork or pass through a sieve, then stir back into the soup, this thickens the broth. Add the {{pasta-dried}} and simmer for the time indicated on the packet, stirring occasionally. The soup should be thick and the pasta tender but not soft. Rest for 5 minutes. Serve with a drizzle of good olive oil, a grind of {{black-pepper}}, and grated {{parmesan}}.

items.fix: Add more stock or water; stir from the base, the starch from the pasta and beans means the bottom scorches quickly if unattended
items.cause: Pasta absorbed too much liquid while simmering
items.symptom: Soup has dried out and pasta is stuck to the base

items.fix: Mash more beans and stir through; or cook uncovered for 5 minutes to reduce the liquid
items.cause: Not enough beans mashed or too much liquid
items.symptom: Soup is too thin and watery

items.fix: Undercook the pasta in the soup by 2 minutes. It continues to cook from the residual heat
items.cause: Pasta cooked to the packet time plus resting
items.symptom: Pasta is overcooked by the time it reaches the table

items.fix: Season assertively, beans are bland and need more salt than you think; the rind adds depth unavailable from stock alone
items.cause: Insufficient seasoning or parmesan rind not used
items.symptom: Soup tastes flat despite all the ingredients

content.text: Pasta e fagioli is the most universally eaten of the cucina povera dishes. It is associated with every region of Italy where beans are grown, which is most of them, and in each region it looks and tastes slightly different. The Venetian version (pasta e fasoi) is almost a broth; the Roman version is thick enough to stand a spoon in. What they share is the logic: dried or tinned beans, small pasta, aromatics, a pork product for savouriness, and a cheese rind dropped in while it cooks. The dish is neither elegant nor quick, but it feeds many people from economical ingredients in a single pot, which is the definition of cucina povera.
